condensed_reasoning:
## Research

I treated this as a **discrete measurement of a monotonically increasing quantity**: NIFC's year-to-date acres-burned figure as it will display on July 24, 2026. I dispatched a research sub-agent with two targeted queries — (1) the latest 2026 NIFC YTD figures and daily burn rate, and (2) the historical July 10→24 YTD increment across 2016–2025.

The research returned excellent primary-source data, all traced to NIFC IMSR PDFs:
- **Latest confirmed figure: 3,578,017 acres** (July 12, 2026 IMSR, through July 11), running at **137% of the 10-year average**, with **44 large fires**, **PL4**, and a western heat dome in the forecast.
- The July 10 anchor was **3,445,499 acres**, implying a lumpy +132,518-acre one-day YTD jump (partly from perimeter re-mapping, since the "acres yesterday" small tally was only 7,060).

## Key Evidence

I built the crux dataset — the empirical **July 10→24 national YTD increment** for each year 2016–2025 — by parsing IMSR PDFs directly in the sandbox and cross-validating:

| Year | Increment |
|------|-----------|
| 2016 | +619k | 2017 | +1,508k | 2018 | +580k | 2019 | +946k | 2020 | +299k |
| 2021 | +882k | 2022 | +774k | 2023 | +116k | 2024 | +763k | 2025 | +839k |

This yields **mean ~732k, median ~768k, std ~359k acres**, strongly right-skewed (range 116k in quiet 2023 to 1.51M in extreme 2017).

## Conclusion

I ran a Monte Carlo (N=200,000) bootstrapping the empirical increments with kernel smoothing (bw ≈ 0.4·std). Because 2026 is running hot, I **downweighted the two quiet-season years** (2020, 2023) to weight 0.4, applied a modest **+5% tilt**, and floored increments at 180k. Applied to the 3.445M July-10 anchor, this produced a right-skewed distribution: **median ~4.26M, mean ~4.30M**, P10 ≈ 3.86M, P90 ≈ 4.85M, P99 ≈ 5.25M. Lower tail bounded near 3.6M (the metric already stands at 3.578M and only rises); upper tail reaches ~5.2M in a 2017/2022-style megafire-run scenario. I set P(<3.475)≈0 and P(>5.525)≈0. My final submission centered the forecast at **~4.26M acres** with a 1st–99th percentile span of roughly 3.63M–5.25M.

subtle_bugs: The large +132,518-acre July 10->11 YTD jump vs. the 7,060-acre 'acres yesterday' small tally reflects perimeter re-mapping absorbed into YTD; the agent noted this lumpiness but did not model its effect on the tail width.

classification.approach: Monte Carlo bootstrap of the empirical 2016-2025 July 10->24 NIFC YTD acre increment, applied to the 2026 July-10 anchor with hot-season reweighting and a modest upward tilt.
classification.key_uncertainty: Whether the forecast western heat dome triggers a 2017/2022-style megafire run (large upside) or fires stay relatively contained over the two-week window.

risk_flags.evidence: The question asks for the total 'per NIFC's year-to-date total published on July 24, 2026.' The agent anchored the historical increment window on the July 10 IMSR value and computed a July 24 increment, which correctly targets the published-July-24 figure. However, NIFC's July 24 IMSR reports figures 'through July 23,' and the agent did not explicitly reconcile whether the resolution refers to the IMSR published on July 24 or the statistics-page value displayed on that date. The empirical increments were built on the same-day IMSR convention (Jul10->Jul24 IMSR-to-IMSR), so the mapping is internally consistent, but the one-report-day ambiguity is unaddressed.

The Monte Carlo compresses the lower tail sharply (P1=3.625, P5=3.680, P10=3.863) by downweighting quiet years and flooring at 180k. Given day-to-day YTD deltas are 'lumpy' and NIFC revises figures, a slightly wider lower tail may be warranted; the empirical P10 increment was 281k (=>3.73M), yet the model's P10 outcome sits at 3.86M, implying the reweighting materially narrowed the left tail.

strengths: Built the crux dataset from primary sources by parsing NIFC IMSR PDFs for all 10 years (2016-2025) in the sandbox and cross-validating, rather than trusting a single summary figure.
Correctly anchored the historical increment window (July 10->24) on the same July 10 base as the 2026 anchor (3.445M), making the increment distribution directly applicable.
Ran a 200K-sample Monte Carlo with kernel smoothing and explicit right-skew, appropriately reflecting the heavy variance (116k to 1.51M) of the July increment.
Recognized the monotonic-increase floor: the metric already read 3.578M through July 11, so the lower bound near 3.475M is essentially unreachable.

Justified the hot-season upward tilt with concrete 2026 signals (137% of 10yr avg, PL4, 44 large fires, heat dome forecast) and specifically downweighted the two identifiable quiet-season analogs (2020, 2023).
weaknesses: The +5% hot-conditions tilt and the 0.4 quiet-year downweight are ad hoc adjustments applied on top of the empirical distribution without sensitivity analysis of how much they shift the median.

Did not explicitly reconcile whether the resolution value is the IMSR-published July 24 figure vs. the nifc.gov statistics-page value on that date, though the increment convention used is internally consistent.
Lower-tail compression (P10 outcome 3.86M) is tighter than the raw empirical increment P10 (281k => 3.73M outcome) would suggest, driven by the reweighting/floor choices.

evidence: Session dated 2026-07-13; all 2026 data points are as-of July 12, 2026 or earlier (July 6/8/11/12).
The July 24, 2026 target date is in the future relative to the session; the agent forecasts it via historical increments rather than citing any July 24 value.
Historical July 24 values used are all from 2016-2025, prior to the session date.
